title: Performance analysis of classical adiabatic annealing on Ising machines
authors: Jacob Lamers, Guy Verschaffelt, Guy Van der Sande
abstract: Ising machines are a promising approach to solve combinatorial optimization problems. They map these problems onto the Ising model and search for low-energy configurations. However, navigating the rugged energy landscapes of these systems remains difficult. To improve this navigation, classical adiabatic annealing has been proposed in the literature as a heuristic optimization method for classical Ising machines. Using this technique, the Hamiltonian of the Ising machine is gradually transformed from an easily solvable Hamiltonian to the target Hamiltonian. However, its purported effectiveness is primarily motivated by an analogy to quantum adiabatic annealing, and systematic benchmarking has remained limited.   In this work, we analyze the classical adiabatic annealing technique using continuation methods. Motivated by insights from this analysis, we propose an optimized annealing strategy we refer to as hybrid classical adiabatic annealing. We benchmark our proposed strategy using MaxCut instances with up to 800 spins and problems with external fields, for which it achieves a marginal improvement for a limited set of problems. We conclude that, although theoretically motivated and occasionally beneficial, the hybrid strategy does not offer a sufficient practical advantage over simpler, existing techniques.
summary: This work rigorously analyzes classical adiabatic annealing applied to Ising machines for solving hard combinatorial optimization problems. The authors propose and test a hybrid annealing strategy designed to navigate the system's energy landscape by avoiding mathematical singularities (bifurcations). Ultimately, they conclude that while theoretically motivated, this advanced technique offers only marginal practical gains over simpler, established simulation methods.
main_problem: The paper analyzes the performance of classical adiabatic annealing (CAA) for solving combinatorial optimization problems mapped onto Ising machines.
main_result: While hybrid CAA shows marginal improvements over simpler techniques like Regular Annealing, its practical advantage diminishes when compared against enhanced baselines such as the spin sign method.
method: The authors use continuation methods to analyze the fixed points of the system's evolution equation while gradually transforming the Ising Hamiltonian from an initial solvable state to the target problem Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The model is an Ising Machine (IM) whose dynamics are governed by a time-dependent Hamiltonian. The process involves adiabatically evolving the coupling matrix and external fields using an interpolation parameter F, which is analogous to quantum adiabatic annealing.
key_observables: Spin amplitudes ($s_i$), ground state energy, Time-to-Target (TTT), and Success Rate (SR).
important_parameters: Annealing parameters like the rate of change in $F$ ($v_F$) and $ eta$ ($v_ eta$), noise strength ($\gamma$), and the initial coupling strength ($ eta_{start}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the system can follow the instantaneous fixed point during annealing, though this is challenged by saddle-node bifurcations (SNs) which necessitate a hybrid two-stage approach.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the evolution of spin amplitudes and energy during CAA, showing how SNs limit direct adiabatic following. Later figures compare TTT across various annealing schedules (hybrid vs. regular) for MaxCut and Beasley instances.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first introducing the theoretical framework of CAA on IMs, then detailing the necessary hybrid strategy to avoid bifurcations, followed by extensive benchmarking comparing this hybrid approach against simpler methods using metrics like TTT across various problem types.
why_interesting: Although focused on classical optimization, the underlying mathematical machinery—analyzing dynamical systems stability (bifurcations) during a controlled parameter sweep—is highly relevant to understanding non-equilibrium dynamics in complex quantum or classical many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Typical example of the evolution under classical adiabatic annealing. Continuation as the interpolation parameter F varies from zero to one of (a) the spin amplitudes of problem g05 60.3 for α = 0 and β = 0.25, (b) spin amplitude 6 in the black rectangle of (a), (c) the energy calculated using the target coupling matrix. (d) Zoom of the energy evolution in the black rectangle of (c). The energy of the ground state of the binary Hamiltonian is represented by the gray dashed line. Solid (dashed) lines are used when the spin amplitude is part of a stable (unstable) state. SN (green and yellow dots) saddle node bifurcation

Fig. 3: Critical β values for problem g05 60.3. βcrit as a function of the interpolation parameter F for α = 0. βmin is defined by adding 0.02 to the maximum βcrit value over all F values and is indicated by the orange line.

Fig. 2: Positions of the saddle node bifurcations for problem g05 60.3(a) The value of the interpolation parameter F of the two saddle node bifurcations (SNs) as a function of the coupling strength β. The Cusp point is indicated by a black dot, annotated with CP 1. (b)-(d) The evolution of spin amplitude 6, used as a proxy of the fixed point, as a function of F for three different fixed β values (0.22, 0.235 and 0.25). These β values are indicated by the black dashed lines in (a). The blue and orange dots represent the SNs. These results were obtained using a constant linear gain α = 0.

Fig. 4: Continuation of CAA using βmin followed by RA for problem g05 60.3 with α = 0.(a): evolution of all spin amplitudes as the interpolation parameter F is increased from zero to one for a fixed β = βmin = 0.222. (b) application of RA to the final state of panel (a). The energy calculated using the target coupling matrix for both (c) CAA and (d) RA. The energy of the ground state of the binary Hamiltonian is represented by the gray dashed line. The region where the fixed point corresponds to this ground state is indicated by the gray background.

Fig. 5: Parameter scan of the hybrid CAA method for an adiabatic easy problem. (a) success rate (SR) and (b) time- to-target (TTT) as a function of the annealing speed vβ and the adiabatic annealing speed vF for problem g05 60.3, fixed linear gain α = −3.5 and noise strength γ = 0.1. A TTT of infinity is colored white.
